sentences.text: My name is Helen Jonas Rosenzweig.
J-O-N-A-S R-O-S-E-N-Z-W-E-G.
Helena Sternlicht.
April 25th, 1925.
In Cracow, Poland.
Szymon Sternlicht.
Lola Sternlicht.
Sydell and [INAUDIBLE].
Sydonia.
I'm 71 years old.
As the most loving, warm, sweet, the best mother I could think of.
She brings warm memories to my life.
She was an inspiration.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I sort of follow her steps.
She was a giving person, helping people in need, always smiling, full of fun.
Those are my memories [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of my mother.
She would top-- dress in clothes like Charlie Chaplin on Purim, and go around and amuse people.
She would go to a Jewish theater, come back, and replay, actually, the play.
She used to make sounds in her mouth with closed mouth, sounded like a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] instrument-- musical instrument-- like a mandolin, while my father would whistle.
It was a happy home.
That's what I remember about my childhood memories.
My father was a very gentle, loving man, very soft spoken.
Exceptionally good natured man.
Very, very good looking.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People that worked for him respected him.
He was very good to them.
And he was the most wonderful father, always telling me stories that amused me.
And I miss them a lot, both of them.
They were the greatest parents one could have had.
But unfortunately it was just a very short time for me to enjoy my parents.
[PAUSES FOR 28 SECONDS]
Oh, yes.
We lived in the suburbs where I was born, outside Cracow.
And my parents decided to move back to town, in Cracow, because of my father's shop was too far to commute.
And at the same time, I started public school.
It was a nice experience.
From the beginning, when we lived in the suburbs, we used to go to school, and I was driven by a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] you know, I was commuting, actually.
But it was a very short time because, short afterwards, we moved to-- to Cracow, [NON-ENGLISH] where we lived.
It was very close to the river and the famous Castle Wawel that everybody knew about.
And it was a fairly new building.
And I adjusted rather quickly.
I made friends.
And we were very happy there.
It was a nice neighborhood.
I was going frequently to the river with my parents, and also to Wawel, which was a famous place for people who came to visit.
It was a historical place.
It was a castle.
There was a lot of history to it.
And me and my sisters would go there frequently, and I went there with my parents.
As a matter of fact, I had one picture taken with my mother that was given to us here by our cousin on this famous castle named Wawel, which I will show you later.
Yes.
Well, Cracow was a historical place.
There were a lot of lovely places to go to.
I was rather young to remember every place I went to.
This Wawel was-- stood out in my mind because it was a place where we-- everybody would would want to go and see.
There were other historical places that we-- I have seen and I visited.
It-- it was a beautiful city.
Cracow was actually the capital of Poland, but in the late-- lately, Warsaw became the new capital.
But Cracow was the historical capital of Poland.
And it was rather nice.
I mean, it...
There were beautiful places to go to and to see.
I walked to school.
It was quite a distance.
I walked to a park, a beautiful park.
And they called it [?
Plante ?].
And daily walk back and forth with my friends.
And supposedly, from what I remember, I did very well.
I was a good student.
As a matter of fact, my mother kept my report card under her pillow when he-- she was dying, which we found later, because she was so proud of me.
I had straight A's, and I received an honorary tablecloth that was embroidered by the seventh graders.
And I was one that received that tablecloth.
And when my mother passed away, they found, in a hospital, under her pillow, my report card with the tablecloth.
So it was rather important to her.
I followed my oldest sister's steps.
She was exceptionally bright, and I-- I just wanted to be like her.
My oldest sister, Sydell.
Everybody would say, you are Sydell's sister?
You are Sydell's sister?
Well, naturally I-- I had to follow her step.
And I was rather diligent, I would say.
I tried hard by-- by achieved what I was after.
[LAUGHS] And I enjoyed it.
I enjoyed school very much.
I always wanted to be a nurse.
I-- I really felt that this was my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] future, and helping people.
And, as it turned out to be, my daughter became a nurse.
So-- and later on, I-- I did follow a similar, similar profession that would be in the health field.
But naturally, at the time, [SIGHS] what happened afterwards, I-- [INAUDIBLE] never came through and I couldn't possibly go to school for nursing or whatever because time have changed.
Very similar.
Just having fun.
We-- you have to understand that in Poland, a child of 12, 13, 14, is really a child.
Is not a grown up, like teenagers here.
We are really-- especially with me.
I was the youngest one home, and I was called the child.
They wouldn't even call me by my name.
They would call me child, the baby, you know, where is the baby?
And I was babied by my parents and by my grandmother.
And I felt like a child.
I-- I felt a happy child.
I was always dancing.
I was always singing.
It was just the type of -- of happy home that I came from.
I was rather happy.
I-- I remembered myself not walking, but hopping and dancing coming back home.
And my mother sometimes would tell me, don't jump, don't run, don't jump.
I mean, she was probably worried.
I was rather skinny little girl.
And-- but that was me.
I was a happy child.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a happy home.
It was a happy, loving home.
We were very close.
Holidays, they always stay in my mind because those were the greatest memories.
Friday night, Saturday.
My mother baking, already, starting baking on Thursday for [INAUDIBLE] and a couple of cakes.
And there was the ritual Friday night dinner.
My father making kiddush.
And it was all-- the family was together.
There were always some friends of my sisters joining.
And it was a-- Saturday was a rest day.
And it-- it was just a happy, happy time of my life.
I think Passover, because it was my-- my birthday used to come out on Passover.
And it was just a double celebration because, naturally, you always used to get new clothes for each holiday.
But I used to be treated even more so because of my birthday.
Well, a lot of nice things.
Nothing as elaborate that we buy for our grandchildren now, but there was always something special that meant to me.
And it's-- it's hard even to remember exactly what it was, but I know that it was always something special given to me by my parents, my grandmother, my aunts.
[SIGHS] It's-- thinking back about those times, it's incredible how happy they were.
And it was just too bad they were so short-lived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But they left me with a very happy outlook in life, in spite of everything, that I am counted.
You know, I do have this in-- this urge for life and-- and it must be-- have a lot to do with my upbringing, I think.
Yes, I shared my room with my sisters.
We-- we didn't have such elaborate home.
I shared with my two sisters.
I remember my corner where I had my bed and my dolls and my carriage.
And it wasn't a home like I live in now, but it was a love-- loving home.
It was a cozy home.
It was my home.
We had, you know-- well, like, three rooms and a kitchen, with a dining room and my parent's bedroom and our bedroom.
And a very large kitchen, which we spent most of the time.
Very cheerful, very warm.
And those are my happy memories.
And prior to it, where I was born as a little girl, I-- we lived mostly between non-Jewish people.
There were only three Jewish families which we were very close to, but my-- my-- my parents were very close with the-- with the Gentile neighbors.
I remember a lot of them coming into our home and having tea and cookies with my mother.
And I-- I played with the children.
As it turned out to be, later, I was very disappointed because they didn't accept us as our-- as friends anymore.
But as a child, I did have pleasant memories.
And those few families, those Jewish families that we lived with, none of them are alive.
I never seen them after the war anymore.
I searched and asked but no one is around.
So--
I remember when we were in the ghetto and-- and when the deportations started to become so frequent, my oldest sister, Sydell, tried to take my mother out of the ghetto and save them--
Before the ghetto?
I remember my parents always worrying about my oldest sister going out at night, not to be too safe for her.
She was already a teenager, and she would come-- go out with her friends.
And-- and I-- I remember, as a child, hearing my parents wondering if she will come home safe.
And that-- I thought, why are they worried?
Well, they were worried because the antisemitism in Poland was rather severe.
And, you know, no one was really that safe.
So I have noticed my parents being concerned about my sister staying out or-- or going in the neighborhood where maybe it-- it wasn't so safe for her.
Do you remember, at that time, anybody being mean to you, or--
Not to me, no.
I did not experience anything like that as a child.
No.
I called myself a child because I felt I was a child, yes.
I-- I personally, now, I don't remember anything that would be directed toward me.
No.
No.
But all around the situation, you could hear my parents talking or I could overhear their conversation about safety, and their wishes always to go to [INAUDIBLE] Israel.
There was always this hope and this wishes on New Year's, where they would say, 'Next year in [?
Erit ?] Israel.' They were not-- I didn't feel that they were safe.
I didn't think they felt as secure in Poland.
Those were my-- my observation as a child.
This is what I can recall.
So it was always this wish of -- of going to [?
Erit ?] Israel.
Yes, of course I knew.
That-- that what we [SIGHS] should have had, that probably this wouldn't have happened if we would have [?
Erit ?] Israel.
But we-- nobody cared.
We were orphans.
Nobody really cared about us.
Maybe if [?
Erit ?] Israel would be in existence, this wouldn't have happened.
I wouldn't lose my parents and my whole family and my dearest friends.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I'm sure it wouldn't have happened.
Of course.
I remember very well.
It was in September 1939.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] You want me to tell my stories, [? is it? ?] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You know, I'm not a public speaker.
I speak from my heart and those are my recollections.
I'm a survivor, and I'm compelled to speak out as a live witness to convey to you my innermost, fearful and painful experiences from the brutal Nazi prosecution.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know now that there must have been a purpose for me to survive, because it wasn't the smartest or the richest or the healthiest.
There was no rhyme or reason.
But it had to be in God's hands to carry me through to tell what happened, and speak for the people that perished in the Holocaust as well.

And I know I have an obligation to see to it that a history of those tragic, dark days will be recorded forever, and carried from generation to generation, and hopefully to save the world from another Hitler.
Yes, I did, because prior to it, about a year prior to 1939, a family from Germany came to us and my parents took 'em in-- into our apartment.
They escaped Germany.
And it was rather crowded in our apartment, but my parents felt they had to take 'em in.
So we knew that Jews in Germany were not safe anymore.
But we never thought that this would happen in Poland.
I don't remember [INAUDIBLE] now.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] You know, I don't think that anybody would ever believe that any human body could or would go as far as the Nazis did.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As a matter of fact, in the documents that were available to us after the war, Hitler's-- Hitler's plan for the Final Solution for the Jewish people was prepared side by side with a plan to conquer other nations.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it all started in September, 1939.
Playing outside.
I came from school and I was playing outside.
And suddenly we heard the march and the planes, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and we heard the Germans are here.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And right from the start, the German ordered forced labor.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my very happy childhood and my safe home life was broken up.
Yes.
I-- I just came from school, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and we were playing in the street.
As a matter of fact, it was my-- you know, school, the public school, was over already.
But I was still going to [INAUDIBLE], which was a Jewish school.
And I-- I was playing with my friends and we heard the march, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I found out I hear the Germans are here.
They just marched into Poland, to Cracow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But nobody expected anything will harm us.
I-- just the usual.
I mean, nobody expected anything goin' to happen to us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Excuse me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They hunt for the Jews in the streets-- easily recognizable because we had to wear a yellow Star of David-- yellow armband-- Star of David on our arms.
Removal of the armband was punishable by death.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They forced us to clean their homes, barracks, offices, clean their cars, sweep the streets, shovel snow later.

They enjoyed seeing very well dressed women to do very heavy work, and they forced very religious Jews to pray and sing and dance in the streets while they were cutting their beards and their earlocks.
New regulation came out limiting free movements for the Jewish people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were not allowed to use public transportation.
And we were not allowed to use any educational facilities.
Everything stopped.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All the synagogues were turned to barracks and warehouses.
All the beautiful belongings were stolen by the Germans.
All the large businesses-- big businesses-- were taken away from Jews, with the exception of small stores like coffee houses.
Small businesses.
They had to display the Star of David in front of the store for the German to be able to loot and steal.
All the bank accounts were frozen.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Life became very treacherous.
They came storming in our homes, always two, three.
Armed.
They would take anything they wanted-- all the precious things in our homes-- while causing a lot of terror.
Walking in the street was dangerous.
This lasted till 1941.
In March of 1941, they ordered us to go to the ghetto.
Those were the boundaries of the Jewish sector.
Yes.
We lived in our home.
We lived in our home.
With my two sisters and my parents.
And we all had to go to work, as I said before.
They assigned us to various jobs.
Was it to sweep the streets or clean office-- a German office, or barrack or-- whatever they ask us to do, we had to do it.
We-- we had-- we had received identification cards, and they had to be stamped each time you arrived to work, so-- and they constantly had searched us and the documents were being inspected.
So we had to work, and the rations were very limited.
And it was very hard.
The very few months in the ghetto, we felt a certain amount of relaxation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We started to go-- I started to go to school.
We had a school.
We had a synagogue.
We had--
